articles.title: Artemis II: A Big Step Toward the Moon
articles.summary: Artemis II is a NASA mission that launched on April 1st with four astronauts: Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen. The mission uses a big rocket called SLS and a spacecraft named Orion. In the first six days, everything worked very well. The rocket had strong power, and the Orion capsule flew smoothly to the Moon without needing many corrections. This success gives people hope and shows that NASA is learning to launch rockets more often. The mission helps test if humans can land on the Moon again by 2028, which is a big goal. The astronauts' actions inspire the world, making this an exciting space adventure.

articles.title: NASA's Amazing Moon Photos: Earthset and Solar Eclipse
articles.summary: NASA has shared new pictures from the Artemis II astronauts who flew by the Moon. One photo shows an 'Earthset,' where Earth looks like it's setting behind the Moon's rocky surface. Another photo shows a solar eclipse, where the Moon blocks the Sun, making a bright ring called the corona. The astronauts took these photos during their trip back to Earth. They saw the eclipse for almost an hour because they were close to the Moon. These photos remind people of the famous Earthrise photo from 1968. NASA says the photos help us learn about space and see how beautiful our planet is from far away.

articles.title: Vitamin D and Brain Health: A Simple Look
articles.summary: A new study looked at how vitamin D might help our brains stay healthy as we get older. Vitamin D is something we get from sunlight and some foods. Researchers followed 793 adults who were about 39 years old and did not have dementia. They checked their vitamin D levels in the blood. About 16 years later, they did brain scans to see proteins called tau, which are linked to dementia. They found that people with higher vitamin D levels in midlife had lower tau levels in their brains later. This means vitamin D might help protect the brain. But the study says it shows a relationship, not proof that vitamin D directly reduces tau or dementia risk. More research is needed to be sure. The study was done by scientists from the University of Galway in Ireland and published in a journal called Neurology Open Access.

articles.title: Scientists Watch Alzheimer's Damage Happen Live
articles.summary: Scientists at Oregon State University have found a new way to watch Alzheimer's disease damage happen in real time. Alzheimer's is a brain disease that affects memory and thinking in older people. In this disease, proteins in the brain clump together and block communication between brain cells. The scientists used a special method to see how metals like copper make these proteins clump. They also saw how certain molecules called chelators can stop or even reverse the clumping. This discovery could help make better medicines for Alzheimer's in the future. The study was led by Professor Marilyn Rampersad Mackiewicz and involved undergraduate students. It gives hope that brain damage from Alzheimer's might be reversible with the right treatment.

articles.title: A Special Gene Helps Pancreatic Cancer Spread
articles.summary: Scientists found a gene called KLF5 that helps pancreatic cancer spread in the body. This gene doesn't change the DNA code but changes how DNA is packaged, which turns genes on or off. Using a tool called CRISPR, they turned off genes to see which ones stop cancer growth, and KLF5 was the most important. In patients, KLF5 was more active in spreading tumors than in the original ones. This gene controls other genes that help cancer cells grow and resist treatment. The research suggests new ways to treat cancer by targeting KLF5, and some drugs are already being tested. This study was done at Johns Hopkins Medicine and published in a science journal.

articles.title: Scientists Find a Hidden Nutrient That Helps Our Brain and Fights Cancer
articles.summary: Scientists have solved a big mystery about a special nutrient called queuosine that our bodies need to stay healthy. This nutrient comes from foods and bacteria in our gut, but for over 30 years, no one knew how it gets into our cells. A team of researchers from places like the University of Florida and Trinity College Dublin found a gene called SLC35F2 that acts like a door to let queuosine into cells. This discovery is important because queuosine helps our brain work better for memory and learning, and it can also fight cancer. The scientists hope this will lead to new treatments in the future. They worked together with support from health organizations to make this breakthrough.
